title: String literals should not be concatenated implicitly
description:
String literals should not be concatenated implicitlyAnalyze your codeconsistency-formattedmaintainabilityCode SmellconfusingWhy is this an issue?While in C, and derived languages, it is legal to concatenate two literals by putting them next to each other, this is only justified in a few cases. For instance if one is a macro or if the layout makes it clearer.Noncompliant code exampleconst char * v1 = "a""b"; // Noncompliant; same as "ab" const char * v2 = "a
" "b
"; // NoncompliantCompliant solutionconst char * v1 = "ab" const char * v2 = "a
" "b
";Exceptionsconst char * v3 = "a" /* comment */ "b"; #define _s "b" const char * v4 = "a" _s; // concatenation with macro ignoredAvailable In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Clo
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
const char * v1 = "a""b";      // Noncompliant; same as "ab"
const char * v2 = "a\n" "b\n"; // Noncompliant

char message[] = "Hello " "World";  // Noncompliant: implicit concatenation
char path[] = "/usr/local" "/bin";     // Noncompliant
compliant:
const char * v1 = "ab";           // Compliant: single literal
const char * v2 = "a\nb\n";       // Compliant: single literal

// Use explicit concatenation or line continuation
char message[] = "Hello World";     // Compliant
char path[] = "/usr/local/bin";      // Compliant

// Acceptable with comments or macros
const char * v3 = "a" /* comment */ "b"; // Exception
#define SUFFIX "_suffix"
const char * v4 = "prefix" SUFFIX;        // Exception

checks.message: String literals should not be concatenated implicitly
